title: Carreg Goch (Wellington MF509) Wellington Memorial
location_name: Carreg Goch (Wellington MF509), Brecon Beacons (Bannau Brycheiniog)
memorial_type: Memorial cairn; aircraft wreckage remains on mountainside
narrative: Almost exactly two and a half years after the R1465 crew died on Waun Rydd, another all-Canadian crew from the same unit -- 22 OTU at Wellesbourne Mountford -- met the same fate in the Brecon Beacons. On the night of 20 November 1944, Wellington MF509 was on a cross-country navigation exercise when the starboard Hercules XVI engine developed problems. Sergeant Burke, the wireless operator, requested permission to descend below the cloud base. But the aircraft had entered shower clouds that deposited heavy layers of ice on the wings, and with insufficient power from the port engine alone, the Wellington lost height and crashed into the south-west slopes of Carreg Goch. All six crew -- known as "the Alouettes" -- were killed. The youngest were Burke and Dusablon, both just 20 years old. The oldest was F/O Allison, the bomb aimer, at 28. The Canadian families knew nothing of the crash circumstances or location for over 60 years. In 2005, cooperation between McGill University in Montreal and the people of the Swansea Valley brought the story to light. In 2006, Wes Cross of McGill placed a Canadian flag at the crash site. In 2013, Phyllis Allison, sister of bomb aimer William, visited the site -- an extraordinary journey of remembrance nearly 70 years after her brother's death.
cause_of_loss: Starboard Hercules XVI engine developed trouble; aircraft entered shower clouds; heavy icing on wings; insufficient power from port engine; loss of height
memorial_details: A stone and concrete memorial cairn marks the centre of the crash site on Carreg Goch. Two plaques are attached: an older plaque listing the six crew names, and a more recent 2006 addition commemorating the visit by Phyllis Allison and the McGill University connection. The memorial is sometimes adorned with poppy wreaths. Considerable wreckage remains scattered around the site. The crash site is a popular hiking destination, accessible via a loop walk of approximately 8 km from the Blaen-y-Glyn area, graded moderate. The initial climb to the Carreg Goch summit is the most demanding section.

external_narratives.summary: Detailed documentation of the Wellington MF509 crash site on Carreg Goch in the Brecon Beacons. The all-Canadian crew of 22 OTU were on a night cross-country exercise when the starboard Hercules XVI engine failed and the aircraft iced up in shower clouds on 20 November 1944.

Local history site documenting the Wellington MF509 crash and the subsequent reconnection between Canadian families and the people of the Swansea Valley. Correspondence between McGill University in Montreal and locals led to a visit by Phyllis Allison, sister of bomb aimer William Allison, in 2013.
key_details: Phyllis Allison visited crash site 2013, nearly 70 years after brother's death
Canadian flag placed at site by Wes Cross of McGill in 2006

external_narratives.summary: Walking guide to the Carreg Goch Wellington Bomber crash site describing the 8km loop walk from Blaen-y-Glyn and the considerable wreckage still visible at the site, including the memorial cairn with two plaques and poppy wreaths.
